skill.name: stop-slop
skill.description: Remove AI writing patterns from prose. Use when drafting, editing, or reviewing text to eliminate predictable AI tells.
skill.summary: Remove predictable AI writing patterns from prose.
skill.author: Hardik Pandya (https://hvpandya.com)

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, reconnaissance, and cryptography indicators. Manual review found only Markdown formatting, author links, writing-style vocabulary, and a prior audit artifact; no executable code, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: README.md uses fenced code formatting and inline code examples to show the skill folder structure and setup options. These are documentation markers, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks appear in Markdown documentation, including a file tree and inline filenames. No executable script file or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Static Links Flagged as Network Risk
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are a GitHub-hosted README image and author attribution links. They do not initiate requests from executable code or send local data anywhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plain Markdown and frontmatter attribution. There is no code path that performs network access or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Writing Vocabulary Flagged as Security Terms
low_findings.description: Terms such as patterns, structures, remove, and think appear in writing guidance. The context is prose editing, not reconnaissance, cryptography, or unsafe system behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text consistently discusses stylistic patterns in prose. I found no security-sensitive operations tied to these words.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Prior Audit Artifact Flagged as Skill Behavior
low_findings.description: skill-audit-output.json is an audit report artifact, not an active skill instruction or executable component. Its flagged lines describe previous evaluations and marketplace copy.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is JSON report content and contains no executable behavior. The flagged text is explanatory content rather than an instruction to the model or host system.

content.value_statement: AI-assisted drafts often contain predictable phrases, rhythms, and rhetorical setups. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code revise prose into direct, varied, and less formulaic writing.
actual_capabilities: Identifies throat-clearing openers and filler phrases in prose.
Flags formulaic structures such as binary contrasts and dramatic fragments.
Suggests plainer alternatives for common business jargon.
Uses a five-part scoring rubric for directness, rhythm, trust, authenticity, and density.
Provides before-and-after revision patterns for common AI writing habits.
Guides final review before publishing AI-assisted text.
limitations: It does not verify facts, citations, or source quality.
It does not guarantee that text will pass AI detection tools.
It is tuned for professional prose, not poetry or experimental fiction.
It relies on model judgment when applying style rules to unusual contexts.

use_cases.title: Polish Drafts Before Publication
use_cases.description: Remove filler, predictable structures, and inflated phrasing from articles, newsletters, and posts.

use_cases.title: Make Documentation More Direct
use_cases.description: Turn AI-assisted explanations into concise documentation that keeps technical meaning intact.

use_cases.title: Review Executive Communications
use_cases.description: Find vague language and formulaic emphasis before sending memos, updates, or announcements.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply stop-slop rules to this draft. Remove filler phrases, jargon, and obvious AI writing habits while preserving the meaning.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a short draft

prompt_templates.title: Style Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this text for throat-clearing, binary contrasts, dramatic fragments, and rhetorical setups. Return a cleaner version and a short change summary.

prompt_templates.title: Score And Revise
prompt_templates.prompt: Score this draft from 1 to 10 on directness, rhythm, trust, authenticity, and density. Revise any area that keeps the total below 35.
prompt_templates.scenario: Structured review using the skill rubric

prompt_templates.title: Editorial Rewrite
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this long-form draft with stop-slop principles. Keep the argument, remove formulaic AI patterns, vary sentence rhythm, and trust the reader.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced rewrite of a complex document

output_examples.input: Here is the thing: our onboarding process is not broken because people ignore it. It is broken because it asks too much.
output_examples.output: Our onboarding process asks too much, so people ignore it.

output_examples.input: In today's fast-paced landscape, teams need to lean into clarity and navigate uncertainty with confidence.
output_examples.output: Teams need clear decisions when plans change.

output_examples.input: What if the real problem is not productivity? What if it is trust? Think about it.
output_examples.output: Low trust is the productivity problem.

best_practices: Run the skill after the main draft exists, when structure and meaning are stable.
Ask for both a revised version and a short list of removed patterns.
Review technical or legal wording manually after style edits.
anti_patterns: Using the skill as a factual accuracy checker.
Removing every distinctive phrase without considering audience or voice.
Treating a high style score as proof that text is human-written.

faq.question: Does this skill write new content?
faq.answer: It can revise text, but its main purpose is editing existing prose for style patterns.

faq.question: Does it make text undetectable as AI-written?
faq.answer: No. It reduces obvious AI writing patterns, but it cannot guarantee detection outcomes.

faq.question: What kinds of text work best?
faq.answer: It works best on articles, memos, documentation, newsletters, and professional updates.

faq.question: Can it preserve technical meaning?
faq.answer: Yes, but technical edits should be reviewed by someone who understands the subject.

faq.question: How does the scoring rubric help?
faq.answer: The rubric gives a repeatable way to judge directness, rhythm, trust, authenticity, and density.

faq.question: Can teams use this as a style guide?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams can use the rules and examples to review AI-assisted writing consistently.
